overview.whyItMatters: Retrieval quality directly determines RAG output quality. Even the most capable foundation model cannot produce accurate responses if it receives irrelevant or incomplete context. Poor retrieval means the FM lacks the information it needs; excellent retrieval enables accurate, grounded responses that cite your actual documents. The exam tests chunking strategies, embedding models, search techniques, and query optimization—mastering these separates functional RAG from exceptional RAG.

concepts.title: Document Chunking Strategies
concepts.content:
Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller pieces for embedding and retrieval. This seemingly simple step has profound impacts on retrieval quality, and getting it wrong is one of the most common RAG failures.

The fundamental tension is between precision and context. Smaller chunks are more precise—they match queries more specifically—but they may lack the context needed for the FM to understand and use the information. Larger chunks preserve context but may contain irrelevant information that dilutes the semantic signal, causing them to match queries they shouldn't.

Fixed-size chunking splits documents every N tokens regardless of content. This is simple to implement and produces predictable chunk sizes, which helps with embedding model token limits. However, it may split mid-sentence or mid-paragraph, creating chunks that lack coherent meaning. A chunk that starts with 'however, the alternative approach...' without context is difficult for both retrieval and generation.

Semantic chunking respects document structure by splitting at natural boundaries—paragraph breaks, section headings, or sentence endings. This produces coherent chunks but with variable sizes. Very short paragraphs may produce tiny chunks lacking context; dense technical sections may exceed embedding model limits.

Hierarchical chunking addresses the precision-context trade-off by creating multiple levels. Parent chunks contain summaries or entire sections; child chunks contain detailed content. During retrieval, you can match against parents to identify relevant sections, then retrieve children for specific details. The FM receives both the specific match and its surrounding context.

Sliding window chunking adds overlap between adjacent chunks. If a relevant passage spans a chunk boundary, both chunks will contain it. This redundancy improves recall at the cost of increased storage and potential duplicate retrieval results.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases provides configurable chunking with adjustable chunk size and overlap parameters. For custom implementations, you must build your own chunking pipeline—Lambda functions are common for this purpose. Experiment with different chunk sizes (typically 256-1024 tokens) to find what works best for your specific content and queries.
keyPoints: Fixed-size: Simple, predictable, but may split mid-thought
Semantic: Respects document structure, variable sizes
Hierarchical: Parent summaries + child details for context preservation
Sliding window: Overlap chunks to capture boundary content
concepts.examTip: If a question mentions 'context preservation' or 'parent-child relationships,' think hierarchical chunking. 'Simplest approach' points to fixed-size with Bedrock KB defaults.

concepts.title: Embedding Model Selection
concepts.content:
Embedding models convert text into numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning. The quality of these embeddings directly impacts retrieval accuracy—if the embedding model doesn't understand your domain vocabulary or fails to capture nuanced meaning, retrieval will suffer.

Amazon Titan Embeddings is AWS's native embedding model, producing 1536-dimensional vectors. It's a solid general-purpose choice that handles most content types well. Titan Embeddings V2 improved multilingual support and performance on various benchmarks. For most Bedrock Knowledge Bases use cases, Titan Embeddings is the default and recommended choice.

Cohere Embed is available through Bedrock and offers strong multilingual capabilities. If your documents span multiple languages or your users query in various languages, Cohere Embed may outperform Titan. It also tends to perform well on search-specific benchmarks.

Dimensionality trade-offs matter for storage and computation. Higher-dimensional vectors (1536 vs 384) capture more nuanced semantic relationships but require more storage space and computation time. For most applications, the default 1536 dimensions provide a good balance.

Domain evaluation is critical. An embedding model that performs well on general benchmarks may underperform on your specific domain. If your content uses specialized terminology—medical, legal, technical—evaluate embedding quality on representative queries from your domain before committing.

Batch embedding generation matters at scale. When processing thousands of documents, use batch inference rather than individual API calls. This reduces costs and improves throughput. Bedrock supports batch inference for embedding generation.

Embedding model consistency is essential. All documents and all queries must use the same embedding model. You cannot embed documents with Titan and query with Cohere—the vector spaces are incompatible. If you change embedding models, you must re-embed all documents.
keyPoints: Titan Embeddings V2: AWS default, 1536 dimensions, good general-purpose
Cohere Embed: Strong multilingual support, excellent for search tasks
Dimensionality: Higher captures more nuance, costs more storage/compute
Batch generation: Use batch inference for large document sets
concepts.examTip: Titan Embeddings is the default choice unless the question mentions multilingual requirements (Cohere) or domain-specific concerns. Always use the same model for documents and queries.

concepts.title: Vector Search Techniques
concepts.content:
Basic vector search finds documents whose embeddings are most similar to the query embedding. This works well for semantic queries but has limitations—it may miss relevant documents that use different terminology or include important keywords not well-captured by embeddings.

Hybrid search combines vector similarity with traditional keyword matching (typically BM25). The intuition is that some queries benefit from exact keyword matches—searching for a specific error code, product name, or technical term—while others benefit from semantic understanding. Hybrid search fusion algorithms combine scores from both approaches, often outperforming either alone.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases supports hybrid retrieval mode, which combines semantic and keyword search automatically. OpenSearch's neural plugin provides more configurable hybrid search with adjustable weights between vector and keyword scores.

Reranking uses a second model to reorder initial retrieval results. A retrieval model (fast but less accurate) returns candidate documents, then a reranker model (slower but more accurate) scores each query-document pair and reorders by relevance. This two-stage approach is more computationally expensive but significantly improves precision for complex queries.

Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) addresses result diversity. Standard retrieval may return multiple chunks from the same document, all saying similar things. MMR penalizes redundancy, selecting results that are both relevant and diverse. This is particularly valuable when your FM context window is limited and you want to maximize information density.

Filtering constrains results by metadata attributes. Pre-filtering (before vector search) is more efficient but requires indexed filter fields. Post-filtering (after vector search) is simpler but may return fewer results than requested. Most production systems use pre-filtering for common filters like document type or access level.
keyPoints: Pure vector: Semantic similarity only, may miss keyword matches
Hybrid search: BM25 keyword + vector similarity fusion
Reranking: Two-stage retrieval with cross-encoder scoring
MMR: Maximal Marginal Relevance for diverse results
concepts.examTip: Hybrid search is almost always better than pure vector for production RAG. Bedrock KB supports HYBRID retrieval mode. Reranking adds precision but increases latency.

concepts.title: Query Handling Techniques
concepts.content:
Raw user queries often don't match how information is expressed in documents. Query optimization techniques bridge this gap, transforming user input into more effective retrieval queries.

Query expansion adds related terms to broaden retrieval. If a user searches for 'AWS compute options,' expansion might add 'EC2,' 'Lambda,' 'Fargate,' 'ECS'—terms likely to appear in relevant documents even if not in the original query. This can be implemented with synonym dictionaries or by asking an FM to suggest related terms.

Query decomposition breaks complex questions into simpler sub-queries. 'Compare the cost and performance of EC2 vs Lambda for batch processing' contains multiple information needs. Decompose it into: 'EC2 batch processing cost,' 'Lambda batch processing cost,' 'EC2 batch processing performance,' 'Lambda batch processing performance.' Retrieve for each sub-query separately, then combine results.

HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) is a powerful technique for difficult queries. Instead of embedding the query directly, ask an FM to generate a hypothetical document that would answer the query. Embed that hypothetical document and use it for retrieval. This works because the hypothetical document is written in 'document style' rather than 'query style,' often matching actual documents more effectively.

Query reformulation uses an FM to rephrase ambiguous or poorly-formed queries. User queries may be incomplete, misspelled, or phrased as commands rather than questions. An FM can reformulate 'how 2 setup vpc peering' into a cleaner query that retrieves better.

Conversational context matters for multi-turn interactions. A query like 'what about the pricing?' is meaningless without the previous conversation. Maintain conversation history and include relevant context when constructing retrieval queries. This might involve extracting entities from prior turns or including the previous user question.

Step Functions can orchestrate complex query pipelines that combine multiple techniques—expand, decompose, retrieve, rerank, and combine results.
keyPoints: Query expansion: Add synonyms and related terms
Query decomposition: Break complex queries into sub-queries
HyDE: Generate hypothetical answer document for retrieval
Reformulation: Clean up ambiguous or poorly-formed queries

skillExplanations.explanation:
Document segmentation determines retrieval granularity—how specific or broad your retrieval results can be. The right segmentation strategy depends on your content type, query patterns, and FM context limits.

For structured documents with clear hierarchies (manuals, legal documents, technical specifications), semantic chunking that respects section boundaries works well. Each section becomes a coherent chunk, and section headings can serve as metadata for filtering.

For unstructured content (emails, chat logs, meeting transcripts), fixed-size chunking with overlap may be more appropriate since natural boundaries are less clear.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases provides automatic chunking with configurable parameters. You set target chunk size and overlap percentage; Bedrock handles the splitting. For most use cases, starting with defaults (around 300 tokens, 10% overlap) and adjusting based on retrieval quality is a reasonable approach.

Custom chunking pipelines offer more control. Lambda functions triggered by S3 uploads can implement domain-specific logic—splitting code files by function, legal documents by clause, or technical docs by procedure step. This requires more development effort but can significantly improve retrieval for specialized content.

awsServices.whenToUse: Automatic chunking with configurable chunk size and overlap. Handles most document types without custom code.
Custom chunking logic for specialized content types. Semantic boundary detection requiring domain knowledge.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Embedding generation converts your documents into the vector representation that enables semantic search. Quality embeddings are essential—no amount of search optimization can compensate for poor embeddings.

Titan Embeddings V2 is the standard choice for Bedrock workloads. It handles English content well and has improved multilingual capabilities. For most use cases, it's the recommended starting point.

Evaluate embedding quality before production deployment. Create a test set of queries with known relevant documents. Generate embeddings and measure recall@k (how many relevant documents appear in top k results). Compare different embedding models on your specific data—general benchmarks don't always predict domain-specific performance.

Batch embedding generation is essential for efficiency at scale. Processing thousands of documents with individual API calls is slow and expensive. Bedrock supports batch inference, and for custom pipelines, you should batch documents and make fewer, larger API calls.

Embedding caching prevents redundant computation. Store embeddings alongside document IDs so you don't re-embed unchanged documents. When documents update, only re-embed the changed content. This is particularly important for incremental knowledge base updates.

awsServices.whenToUse: Default embedding model for Bedrock workloads. 1536 dimensions, good general-purpose performance.
Access to multiple embedding models including Cohere Embed. Batch inference for efficient large-scale embedding.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Vector search retrieves documents semantically similar to query embeddings. The search implementation significantly impacts retrieval quality and performance.

OpenSearch k-NN provides scalable vector search with HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) indexes. HNSW enables fast approximate nearest neighbor search—it may not find the mathematically exact nearest neighbors, but it finds very close approximations much faster than exact search. For most applications, this trade-off is worthwhile.

Aurora pgvector adds vector operations to PostgreSQL. For teams already using PostgreSQL, pgvector integrates vector search into existing database infrastructure. Query syntax uses familiar SQL with distance operators. pgvector supports both exact and approximate (IVFFlat) search.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases abstracts the search layer entirely. You query the knowledge base; Bedrock handles the vector search internally. This is the simplest option but offers less control over search parameters.

Tuning search parameters affects the precision-recall trade-off. In OpenSearch, the ef_search parameter controls how many candidates HNSW considers—higher values improve recall but slow search. Test different parameter values with your actual queries to find the optimal balance.

awsServices.whenToUse: Large-scale k-NN search with HNSW indexes. Fine-grained control over search parameters and sharding.
Vector search for PostgreSQL workloads. SQL-compatible queries with pgvector extension.
Managed vector search with zero configuration. Simplest option for most RAG applications.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Advanced search techniques improve retrieval precision beyond basic vector similarity. These techniques are particularly valuable for complex queries and high-stakes applications where retrieval accuracy is critical.

Hybrid search fusion combines vector and keyword scores. OpenSearch's neural plugin supports weighted combinations—you can emphasize keyword matching for precise technical queries or semantic similarity for conceptual questions. Bedrock KB's HYBRID mode provides this automatically with sensible defaults.

Reranker models score query-document pairs with more computational power than initial retrieval. Bedrock offers reranker models that can be applied to retrieval results. The pattern is: retrieve top-N candidates with fast vector search, then rerank those N documents with a more accurate (slower) model, returning top-K. This two-stage approach balances efficiency and accuracy.

Cross-encoder reranking is particularly effective because it jointly processes the query and document, capturing relevance signals that separate embedding models miss. The downside is computational cost—cross-encoders cannot be pre-computed and must run at query time.

awsServices.whenToUse: Neural plugin for hybrid search. Custom scoring functions. BM25 + vector fusion.
Reranker models for result reordering. HYBRID retrieval mode for KB queries.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Query handling transforms user input into effective retrieval queries. Raw user queries are often suboptimal for retrieval—they may be ambiguous, incomplete, or phrased differently than document content.

Query expansion uses an FM to add related terms. A query about 'serverless compute' might expand to include 'Lambda,' 'Fargate,' 'event-driven,' and other related concepts. This improves recall by matching documents that use different but related terminology.

Query decomposition handles multi-part questions by breaking them into simpler sub-queries. This is essential for complex analytical queries that span multiple topics. Retrieve for each sub-query, then aggregate results for the FM.

Step Functions orchestrates complex query pipelines. A sophisticated system might: (1) reformulate the query for clarity, (2) expand with related terms, (3) decompose into sub-queries, (4) retrieve for each sub-query, (5) rerank combined results, (6) apply MMR for diversity. Step Functions manages this multi-stage workflow with proper error handling and retry logic.

Conversational query handling requires context awareness. In a multi-turn conversation, queries often reference earlier context ('what about that?'). Include relevant conversation history when constructing retrieval queries.

awsServices.whenToUse: FM-based query expansion, reformulation, and decomposition. Generate hypothetical documents for HyDE.
Orchestrate multi-stage query processing pipelines. Handle complex branching and error recovery.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Access mechanisms provide standardized interfaces between FMs and retrieval systems. These interfaces enable tool use and agentic patterns where the FM decides when and how to retrieve information.

Function calling allows FMs to invoke retrieval tools. Define a retrieval function with parameters (query, filters, top_k), and the FM can call it when it needs external information. This enables more flexible retrieval patterns than always-retrieve approaches.

Bedrock's Converse API supports tools natively. You define tool schemas, and the FM returns tool_use blocks when it wants to invoke a tool. Your application executes the tool and returns results for the FM to incorporate.

Lambda functions serve as flexible API endpoints for retrieval. The FM invokes a Lambda function (via tool calling), Lambda performs the retrieval (querying OpenSearch, Aurora, or Bedrock KB), and returns results to the FM.

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an emerging standard for tool integration. It provides a consistent interface for FMs to interact with external tools and data sources, potentially simplifying multi-tool architectures.

awsServices.whenToUse: Converse API with tool definitions. Function calling for retrieval integration.
Custom retrieval APIs invoked via function calling. Bridge FM tool calls to data sources.

values.Hybrid Search: Mixed queries with keywords and concepts

values.Hybrid + Reranking: High-stakes applications where accuracy justifies latency

examStrategies.approach: Look for hybrid search, reranking, or query expansion in the answer options. These techniques directly improve retrieval precision. Order of impact for most scenarios: hybrid search > reranking > query expansion. If question mentions 'without increasing latency,' favor hybrid over reranking.

Query decomposition breaks complex queries into simpler sub-queries. Step Functions orchestrates the multi-stage processing. Look for answers mentioning decomposition, multi-stage retrieval, or orchestration.
OpenSearch with HNSW indexes enables fast approximate search at scale. Batch embedding generation improves ingestion throughput. Asynchronous processing handles non-real-time retrieval efficiently.

keyTakeaways: Chunking strategy directly impacts retrieval quality—test different sizes with your content
Titan Embeddings V2 is the default choice; evaluate alternatives on your specific domain
Hybrid search (keyword + vector) outperforms pure vector for most production use cases
Reranking improves precision significantly but adds latency—use for high-stakes applications
Query optimization (expansion, decomposition, HyDE) bridges the query-document vocabulary gap
commonMistakes: Using only vector search when hybrid would produce better results
Chunks too large (diluted relevance) or too small (lost context)
Not evaluating embedding models on domain-specific test queries
Skipping query preprocessing for user-facing applications
Assuming retrieval is 'good enough' without measuring recall on test queries
